title: Find majority element
description: Find element that appears more than n by 2 times if it exists

examples.explanation:
Array: [3, 2, 3], n=3, n/2=1
Candidate 3 at index 0: count=0, compare with 3 (index 0) → count=1, compare with 2 (index 1) → no match, compare with 3 (index 2) → count=2. count=2 > 1 → return 3

Array: [2, 2, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2], n=7, n/2=3
Candidate 2 at index 0: count=0, compare with 2 (0) → 1, with 2 (1) → 2, with 1 (2) → no, with 1 (3) → no, with 1 (4) → no, with 2 (5) → 3, with 2 (6) → 4. count=4 > 3 → return 2

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each element, count its occurrences by scanning the entire array; return the element if its count exceeds n/2. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Majority Element Finder
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of brute force O(n²) algorithm to find element appearing more than n/2 times
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the majority element problem by exhaustively checking every element against all other elements. The intuition is straightforward: if an element appears more than n/2 times, then when we count its occurrences by comparing it to every element in the array, we'll find a count greater than n/2. This works because the definition of majority element is purely based on frequency, so we can verify each candidate by counting. The algorithm proceeds in two nested loops: the outer loop selects each element as a candidate, and the inner loop counts how many times this candidate appears in the entire array. For each candidate, we initialize a counter to 0, then iterate through the array, incrementing the counter whenever we find an element equal to the candidate. After counting, if the counter exceeds n/2, we return that candidate immediately. If we complete all candidates without finding a majority element, we return None or -1 to indicate no majority exists. Time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n elements, we perform n comparisons in the worst case. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects directly to the problem statement by implementing the exact definition. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle empty arrays, not returning early when majority is found (wasting computation), incorrectly calculating n/2 (should use integer division), and not considering negative numbers or zeros. The brute force approach is inefficient but serves as a baseline for understanding more optimized solutions.

examples.trace:
Array: [3, 2, 3], n=3, n/2=1
Candidate 3 at index 0: count=0, compare with 3 (index 0) → count=1, compare with 2 (index 1) → no match, compare with 3 (index 2) → count=2. count=2 > 1 → return 3

Array: [2, 2, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2], n=7, n/2=3
Candidate 2 at index 0: count=0, compare with 2 (0) → 1, with 2 (1) → 2, with 1 (2) → no, with 1 (3) → no, with 1 (4) → no, with 2 (5) → 3, with 2 (6) → 4. count=4 > 3 → return 2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Element Frequency Counter
ladders.desc: Count occurrences of a specific element in an array using linear scan
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: given an array and a target element, count how many times the target appears. The intuition is simple: to determine if an element is a majority, we need to know its frequency, which requires comparing it with every element. The algorithm initializes a counter to 0, then iterates through each element in the array. For each element, if it equals the target, we increment the counter. After processing all elements, we return the counter. Time complexity is O(n) for scanning the array once, and space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by being called for each candidate element in the outer loop. Common mistakes include: modifying the array during counting, using the wrong comparison operator (e.g., assignment instead of equality), and not resetting the counter for different targets. This sub-routine is a fundamental pattern in many algorithms where frequency matters.

examples.trace: count=0, element 1 → equals target → count=1, element 2 → not equal, element 1 → equals → count=2, element 3 → not equal → final count=2
count=0, element 5 → equals → count=1, element 5 → equals → count=2, element 5 → equals → count=3 → final count=3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pairwise Comparison for Frequency
ladders.desc: Compare each element with all others to determine if it could be majority
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is the pairwise comparison: for a given candidate element, we check it against every element in the array to count matches. The intuition is that to verify a candidate's majority status, we need to know if it appears more than half the time, which requires examining all elements. This is done by iterating through the array with a nested loop structure: the outer loop picks a candidate, and the inner loop compares this candidate with each element. If a match is found, we increment a counter. After the inner loop completes, we check if the counter exceeds n/2. This logic is central because it directly implements the definition of majority element. Time complexity for this logic on one candidate is O(n), and in the full algorithm, it's applied n times. Space complexity is O(1). It connects to the key sub-routine by being the implementation of counting. Common mistakes include: comparing an element with itself and double-counting (but this is correct as it counts the candidate itself), not using a fresh counter for each candidate, and incorrect loop bounds. This logic is a brute force verification technique used in various problems.

examples.trace: count=0, compare 3 with 3 → match → count=1, compare 3 with 2 → no match, compare 3 with 3 → match → count=2, check: 2 > 1 → true
count=0, compare 1 with 1 → match → count=1, compare 1 with 2 → no match, compare 1 with 3 → no match, check: 1 > 1 → false

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Array with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested loops to compare each element with every other element
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using two loops: an outer loop that iterates through each index i from 0 to n-1, and an inner loop that iterates through each index j from 0 to n-1. For each pair (i, j), we compare the element at index i with the element at index j. The intuition is that to check all possible relationships between elements, we need to consider every pair, which is achieved by nested iteration. In the context of majority element, the outer loop selects a candidate (element at i), and the inner loop counts how many times this candidate appears by comparing with all elements (including itself). This pattern is common in brute force algorithms where O(n²) comparisons are needed. Time complexity is O(n²) as there are n * n iterations, and space complexity is O(1). It connects to the core logic by providing the structural framework for pairwise comparisons. Common mistakes include: using the same variable for both loops causing conflicts, incorrect loop ranges (off-by-one errors), and not handling empty arrays. This building block is fundamental for algorithms like bubble sort or finding duplicates.

examples.trace: i=0: j=0 compare 1-1, j=1 compare 1-2, j=2 compare 1-3; i=1: j=0 compare 2-1, j=1 compare 2-2, j=2 compare 2-3; i=2: j=0 compare 3-1, j=1 compare 3-2, j=2 compare 3-3
i=0: j=0 compare 4-4, j=1 compare 4-4; i=1: j=0 compare 4-4, j=1 compare 4-4

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Equality Check
ladders.desc: Compare two elements to see if they are equal
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves checking whether two given values are equal. In programming, this is typically done using the equality operator (== in many languages). The intuition is fundamental: to count occurrences or find matches, we need to determine if two items are the same. For the majority element problem, this operation is used in the inner loop to compare the candidate element with each array element. The algorithm is simple: take two inputs, a and b, and return true if a equals b, false otherwise. Time and space complexity are O(1) as it's a constant-time comparison. This feeds into the building block by being the action performed in each iteration of the nested loops. Common mistakes include: using assignment (=) instead of equality (==), not considering type mismatches (e.g., comparing integers with strings), and floating-point precision issues. This operation is ubiquitous in algorithms and is a prerequisite for any comparison-based logic.

examples.trace: 5 == 5 → true
3 == 7 → false

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves understanding how to retrieve elements from an array using their positions (indices). An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible via an index starting from 0. The intuition is that to process data in an array, we need to read each element, which requires knowing how to reference them by index. For example, if arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] gives 10, arr[1] gives 20, and arr[2] gives 30. In the majority element problem, we use indexing in loops to traverse the array: for i in range(n): candidate = arr[i]. Time complexity for accessing one element is O(1), and space complexity is O(1) if just reading. This connects to the basic operation by providing the elements to compare. Common mistakes include: using indices out of bounds (e.g., accessing arr[n] when indices go up to n-1), confusing zero-based indexing with one-based, and not initializing arrays properly. This concept is essential for beginners to manipulate collections of data.

examples.trace: Array has indices 0,1,2. Index 1 refers to the second element, which is 6.
Index 0 is the first element, so access gives 1.

approaches.name: HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a hash map to store the frequency of each element while iterating through the array; return the element with frequency greater than n/2. This approach trades space for time efficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Majority Element Using HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find element appearing more than n/2 times using hash map frequency counting.
ladders.explanation: The HashMap approach solves the majority element problem by trading space for time efficiency. The intuition is straightforward: if we count how many times each element appears, we can easily check if any count exceeds n/2. This works because the problem guarantees at most one such element exists (or none). We iterate through the array once, maintaining a hash map where keys are array elements and values are their frequencies. For each element, we increment its count in the map. After processing all elements, we iterate through the map to find any element with frequency > n/2. The time complexity is O(n) because we make two passes through the array (one for counting, one for checking), but each hash map operation is O(1) on average. Space complexity is O(n) in the worst case when all elements are distinct. This connects to L1 by using the frequency counting subroutine as its core component. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling empty arrays, 2) Returning early without checking all elements (can't do this with hash map approach), 3) Using integer division incorrectly for n/2 comparison. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty hash map. 2) For each element in array: if element not in map, add with count 1; else increment count. 3) Calculate threshold = len(array) // 2. 4) For each key in map: if map[key] > threshold, return key. 5) Return None or appropriate value if no majority element found.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Element Frequencies in Array
ladders.desc: Create frequency map counting occurrences of each element in an array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the HashMap approach. The intuition is that many array problems require knowing how many times each value appears, and a hash map provides perfect O(1) average access for this counting task. We iterate through the array once, and for each element, we check if it's already in our map. If not, we add it with count 1. If it exists, we increment its count. This builds a complete frequency distribution. Time complexity is O(n) since we process each element once with O(1) map operations. Space complexity is O(k) where k is the number of distinct elements (at most n). This feeds into L0 by providing the frequency data needed to find the majority element. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to initialize the map, 2) Using wrong increment logic, 3) Not handling the first occurrence specially. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty dictionary. 2) For each element in array: if element not in dictionary, dictionary[element] = 1; else dictionary[element] += 1. 3) Return the dictionary.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Check If Any Frequency Exceeds Threshold
ladders.desc: Given frequency map and threshold, find key with count > threshold.
ladders.explanation: This represents the decision logic of the majority element problem. The intuition is that after counting frequencies, we need to identify if any element appears more than half the time. We calculate threshold = n/2 (using integer division), then iterate through the frequency map to find any element whose count exceeds this threshold. This works because the majority element definition is precisely "count > n/2". Time complexity is O(k) where k is number of distinct elements (≤ n). Space complexity is O(1) additional space. This connects to L1 by taking its frequency map output and applying the majority condition. Common mistakes: 1) Using >= instead of > (must be strictly greater than n/2), 2) Not calculating threshold correctly for odd n, 3) Returning first element that meets condition without checking others (correct here since at most one can satisfy). The algorithm: 1) Calculate threshold = array_length // 2. 2) For each key in frequency_map: if frequency_map[key] > threshold, return key. 3) Return None if no such key found.

examples.trace:
Step 1: threshold = 3 // 2 = 1
Step 2: Check key 3: count 2 > 1 → return 3

Step 1: threshold = 4 // 2 = 2
Step 2: Check key 1: count 2 > 2? No (2 is not greater than 2)
Step 3: Check key 2: count 2 > 2? No
Step 4: No key satisfies → return null

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Through Array and Update Dictionary
ladders.desc: Process each array element, updating a dictionary with element counts.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept combines array iteration with dictionary updates. The intuition is that we need to traverse all elements sequentially while maintaining state (the frequency counts). Dictionaries are ideal because they allow O(1) average time lookups and updates. For each element, we perform a dictionary lookup to see if we've encountered it before. If not, we initialize its count to 1. If yes, we increment the existing count. This pattern appears in many counting problems. Time complexity is O(n) for the array traversal. Space complexity is O(1) for variables plus the dictionary being built. This feeds into L2 by providing the dictionary iteration pattern needed for both building and checking the frequency map. Common mistakes: 1) Using wrong dictionary methods, 2) Not handling the 'first occurrence' case properly, 3) Modifying dictionary while iterating. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty dict. 2) For element in array: if element not in dict: dict[element] = 1; else: dict[element] += 1.

examples.trace:
Step 1: dict = {}
Step 2: Process 7: not in dict → dict={7:1}

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Dictionary Lookup and Insert/Update
ladders.desc: Check if key exists in dictionary and update its value accordingly.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is crucial for building frequency maps. The intuition is that dictionaries store key-value pairs, and we often need to check if a key exists before performing operations. For frequency counting, we need to either initialize a new key with count 1 (if first occurrence) or increment an existing key's value. This requires two basic dictionary operations: 'in' check for existence, and assignment/update of values. Time complexity is O(1) average for both operations. Space complexity is O(1) for the operations themselves. This feeds into L3 by providing the core dictionary manipulation needed during array iteration. Common mistakes: 1) Using wrong syntax for dictionary access, 2) Trying to increment non-existent key (causing KeyError), 3) Confusing dictionary methods. The operation: if key in dict: dict[key] += 1; else: dict[key] = 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Store and Retrieve Values Using Keys
ladders.desc: Basic dictionary operations: store value with key, retrieve value using key.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for the HashMap approach. The intuition is that dictionaries (hash maps) allow us to associate values with unique keys for fast lookup. Think of it like a real dictionary where words (keys) have definitions (values). In programming, we use this to store data in a way that lets us quickly find values by their associated keys. The basic operations are: 1) Storing: dict[key] = value, 2) Retrieving: value = dict[key], 3) Checking existence: key in dict. These operations typically take O(1) average time. This foundational concept feeds into L4 by providing the building blocks for the lookup-and-update pattern. For complete beginners: imagine you're keeping track of how many apples each person has. The person's name is the key, and the number of apples is the value. When someone gets another apple, you look up their name and increase their count. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing keys and values, 2) Using wrong brackets, 3) Trying to retrieve non-existent keys without checking. Example: my_dict = {}; my_dict['alice'] = 3 (store); apples = my_dict['alice'] (retrieve); 'alice' in my_dict returns True (check).

approaches.name: Boyer-Moore Voting O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array, maintaining a candidate and count; update the candidate based on count changes, then verify if it is the majority element. This approach finds the potential majority in linear time with constant space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Majority Element Using Boyer-Moore Voting
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find element appearing more than n/2 times in an array
ladders.explanation: The Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm solves the majority element problem in O(n) time with O(1) space by cleverly tracking a candidate and count. The intuition is that if a majority element exists, it will survive the pairing process where we cancel out non-majority elements against each other. The algorithm works in two phases: first, we find a candidate by iterating through the array, maintaining a candidate and count. When we see the candidate, we increment count; when we see a different element, we decrement count. If count reaches zero, we change the candidate to the current element. This process ensures that if a majority element exists, it will be the final candidate. However, we must verify this candidate by counting its actual occurrences in a second pass, since the algorithm might return a candidate even when no majority exists. The key insight is that majority elements cannot be completely canceled out by non-majority elements because they appear more than half the time. Time complexity is O(n) for two passes through the array, and space complexity is O(1) since we only store a candidate and count. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete voting and verification process. Common mistakes include forgetting the verification step (which can return incorrect results for arrays without majority), or misunderstanding how the algorithm handles ties. The real pattern involves initializing candidate and count, iterating to find candidate, then verifying with a second count.

examples.trace: Phase 1 - Find candidate: Start candidate=None, count=0. Index 0: element=3, candidate=None so set candidate=3, count=1. Index 1: element=2, different from candidate 3, decrement count to 0, count=0 so set candidate=2, count=1. Index 2: element=3, different from candidate 2, decrement count to 0, count=0 so set candidate=3, count=1. Candidate after phase 1: 3. Phase 2 - Verify: Count occurrences of 3: appears at indices 0 and 2 → count=2. n=3, 2 > 3/2=1.5 → majority exists. Return 3.

Phase 1: Start candidate=None, count=0. Index 0: 2, candidate=None → candidate=2, count=1. Index 1: 2, same as candidate → count=2. Index 2: 1, different → count=1. Index 3: 1, different → count=0 → candidate=1, count=1. Index 4: 1, same → count=2. Index 5: 2, different → count=1. Index 6: 2, different → count=0 → candidate=2, count=1. Candidate after phase 1: 2. Phase 2: Count occurrences of 2: indices 0,1,5,6 → count=4. n=7, 4 > 7/2=3.5 → majority exists. Return 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Boyer-Moore Voting Phase
ladders.desc: Implement the voting process to find a potential majority candidate
ladders.explanation: The voting phase is the core of the Boyer-Moore algorithm that finds a candidate for the majority element in a single pass. The intuition is based on the concept of pairing: we maintain a current candidate and a count representing how many unmatched votes it has. When we encounter the same element as the candidate, we increment count (reinforcing the candidate). When we encounter a different element, we decrement count (canceling one vote for the candidate with one vote against). If count drops to zero, we discard the current candidate and start fresh with the new element as candidate. This works because if a majority element exists, it appears more than n/2 times, so even after canceling with all other elements, it will still have votes remaining. The algorithm doesn't guarantee the candidate is actually the majority (that requires verification), but it guarantees that if a majority exists, this candidate will be it. Time complexity is O(n) for one pass, space is O(1). This connects to L0 by providing the candidate finding step, and to L2 which focuses on the count update logic. Common mistakes include not handling the initial case (when candidate is None), or incorrectly updating count. The pseudocode initializes candidate=None, count=0, then for each element: if count==0, set candidate=element, count=1; else if element==candidate, count+=1; else count-=1.

examples.trace: Start candidate=None, count=0. Index 0: element=1, count=0 → candidate=1, count=1. Index 1: element=2, different from candidate 1 → count=0 → candidate=2, count=1. Index 2: element=1, different from candidate 2 → count=0 → candidate=1, count=1. Final candidate: 1.

Start candidate=None, count=0. Index 0: 3 → candidate=3, count=1. Index 1: 3 → same → count=2. Index 2: 4 → different → count=1. Index 3: 4 → different → count=0 → candidate=4, count=1. Index 4: 5 → different → count=0 → candidate=5, count=1. Final candidate: 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Count Update Mechanism
ladders.desc: Implement the logic to update candidate and count based on current element
ladders.explanation: The count update mechanism is the decision logic at each step of the Boyer-Moore algorithm. For each element in the array, we decide whether to reinforce the current candidate, cancel a vote, or switch candidates. The intuition is that we're simulating a voting process where each element gets one vote. If the current element matches the candidate, we increment count (adding a vote for the candidate). If it doesn't match, we decrement count (canceling one vote for the candidate with one vote against). When count reaches zero, it means the current candidate has been completely canceled out by opposing votes, so we switch to the new element as candidate with count=1. This ensures that at any point, count represents how many more times the candidate has appeared than other elements since it was last set. The key insight is that majority elements will survive this cancellation process. This is a standalone sub-problem where given a current candidate and count, and a new element, return the updated candidate and count. Time and space are O(1) per update. This connects to L1 as the inner loop logic, and to L3 which handles array iteration. Common mistakes include incorrect condition ordering or not resetting count properly when switching candidates. The pseudocode: if count==0: candidate=element, count=1; else if element==candidate: count+=1; else: count-=1.

examples.trace: Current candidate=3, count=2. New element=3 matches candidate → increment count to 3. Output candidate=3, count=3.
Current candidate=5, count=1. New element=7 doesn't match → decrement count to 0. Since count=0, we would switch candidate in next step, but for this update output candidate=5, count=0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Array and Track Variables
ladders.desc: Loop through an array while maintaining and updating state variables
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating through an array while keeping track of one or more variables that change based on each element. The intuition is that many algorithms (including Boyer-Moore) require processing each element sequentially and updating state accordingly. Here, we focus on the pattern of initializing variables (like candidate and count), then for each element in the array, applying some logic to update those variables. This is fundamental to linear-time algorithms. The step-by-step approach: 1) Initialize variables before the loop. 2) Use a for-loop to access each element. 3) Inside the loop, apply conditional logic to update variables based on the current element. 4) After the loop, the variables hold the final state. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop, space is O(1) for the variables. This connects to L2 by providing the iteration framework, and to L4 which handles individual comparisons. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop indices, or not initializing variables properly. The pseudocode: candidate=None; count=0; for element in array: [update logic]; return candidate.

examples.trace: Initialize max_val=None. Index 0: element=1, max_val=None → max_val=1. Index 1: element=2, 2>1 → max_val=2. Index 2: element=3, 3>2 → max_val=3. Final max_val=3.
Initialize last=None. Index 0: element=5 → last=5. Index 1: element=3 → last=3. Index 2: element=5 → last=5. Index 3: element=2 → last=2. Final last=2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Update Variables
ladders.desc: Perform conditional checks and update variables based on comparisons
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing values and updating variables conditionally, which is fundamental to decision-making in algorithms. The intuition is that algorithms often need to make choices based on whether two values are equal, greater, or less than each other. In the context of Boyer-Moore, we compare the current element with the candidate to decide whether to increment, decrement, or change count. The operation typically uses if-else statements: check a condition (like element==candidate), and based on the result, update variables (like count+=1 or count-=1). This is a building block for more complex logic. Time and space are O(1). This connects to L3 as the core update step within a loop, and to L5 which handles basic variable operations. Common mistakes include using assignment (=) instead of comparison (==), or not covering all cases. The pseudocode: if condition: variable=new_value; else: variable=other_value.

examples.trace: Compare a=5 and b=3. Since 5>3, set max=5. Output max=5.
Compare x=10 and y=10. They are equal, so set equal=true. Output equal=true.

testCases.input: temp=30, freeze=0, check if above freezing
length=10, max_len=20, check if within limit

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Variables and Conditional Statements
ladders.desc: Understand variables, assignments, and basic if-else conditions
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the absolute basics needed for any programming: variables store data (like numbers or None), and conditional statements (if-else) allow decision-making based on comparisons. The intuition is that algorithms manipulate data through variables and make choices using conditions. In Boyer-Moore, we use variables candidate and count to track state, and if-else to decide how to update them. Variables are like containers that hold values; you can assign values (candidate=3), update them (count+=1), or compare them (element==candidate). Conditional statements execute different code based on whether a condition is true or false. For example, if count==0: candidate=element sets candidate only when count is zero. This is prerequisite for all higher levels. Time and space are O(1). This connects to L4 by providing the syntax for comparisons and updates. Common mistakes include confusing = (assignment) with == (comparison), or not using proper indentation. The pseudocode: variable=value; if condition: do_something; else: do_something_else.

examples.trace: Assign x=5. Condition x>3 evaluates to 5>3 which is true. Output true.

examples.input: Set flag=False, check if flag is True
examples.trace: Assign flag=False. Condition flag==True evaluates to False==True which is false. Output false.

testCases.input: value=None, check if value is None
